title: China can do it for free?
gist: Chinese copper smelters agreed a first-ever zero treatment charge with Chile's Antofagasta for 2026, with spot TCs deep negative, as breakneck smelting capacity expansion vastly outstrips mine supply and shifts pricing power upstream to miners.

insights.claim: Chinese smelters and Chilean miner Antofagasta finalized the full-year 2026 long-term copper concentrate treatment charge at $0 per ton and 0 cents per pound, the first zero processing fee in copper industry history.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A zero benchmark TC signals smelters have lost all pricing power to miners; watch smelter margins and any output discipline that could tighten refined-copper supply.
insights.quote: marking the first zero processing fee in the history of the copper industry
insights.locator: In December 2025, Chinese smelters and Chilean mining firm

insights.claim: The 2026 long-term TC of $0/ton and 0 cents/pound is a sharp plunge from 2025 levels of $21.25 per ton and 2.125 cents per pound.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: The year-over-year collapse in the benchmark quantifies how fast smelter economics have deteriorated, a leading indicator of consolidation or shutdown risk.
insights.quote: a sharp plunge from the 2025 levels of $21.25 per ton and 2.125 cents per pound
insights.locator: This figure represents a sharp plunge

insights.claim: Domestic Chinese spot treatment charges have slumped to -$125 per dry metric ton while spot crude smelting TCs have sunk below -$40 per ton, meaning smelters must pay miners to process ore rather than being paid.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Deeply negative spot TCs invert the entire midstream profit model and pressure smelters that lack by-product buffers toward losses.
insights.quote: domestic spot TC has slumped to -$125 per dry metric ton
insights.locator: Data from Mysteel shows domestic spot TC

insights.claim: Persistent negative treatment charges have completely overturned the smelters' traditional profit model that was built on earning processing fees.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A structural break, not a cyclical dip, means the midstream must be re-underwritten on by-product revenue rather than TC income.
insights.quote: Instead of receiving payment for smelting, smelters must pay miners to process their ore.
insights.locator: Persistent negative TCs have completely overturned

insights.claim: Between 2023 and 2025 China added 2.65 million metric tons per annum of new copper smelting capacity while overseas capacity rose 1.75 million mtpa, far outstripping mine supply growth.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: The scale of Chinese smelter buildout is the root cause of negative TCs; the imbalance persists until capacity is idled or mine supply catches up.
insights.quote: China added 2.65 million metric tons per annum of new copper smelting capacity, while overseas capacity rose by 1.75 million metric tons per annum
insights.locator: Li Chengbin, copper ore analyst at Mysteel

insights.claim: An additional 800,000 metric tons per annum of new copper smelting capacity is projected to come online in 2026, worsening an already severe concentrate shortage.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: More capacity into a concentrate-short market implies TCs stay pinned near or below zero through 2026, keeping the miner-vs-smelter power imbalance intact.
insights.quote: An additional 800,000 metric tons per annum of new smelting capacity is projected to come online in 2026
insights.locator: An additional 800,000 metric tons per annum

insights.claim: Global mine output rose by only roughly 1 million metric tons per annum, a fraction of the smelting capacity added over the same period.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: The gap between ~1 Mtpa mine growth and ~4.4 Mtpa added smelting capacity frames a multi-year structural concentrate deficit favoring upstream miners.
insights.quote: mine output has only increased by roughly 1 million metric tons per annum
insights.locator: By contrast, mine output has only increased

insights.claim: As major mining regions including Chile and Peru mature, ore grades keep declining and operational disruptions grow more frequent, shrinking concentrate supply and handing miners the upper hand in negotiations.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Falling grades in mature districts are a durable supply constraint, supporting elevated copper prices and miner pricing power over smelters.
insights.quote: ore grades at mines continue to decline, operational disruptions have become more frequent, and supply volumes have shrunk

insights.claim: Profit margins across the copper chain have shifted upstream toward mines, giving mining companies their strongest bargaining position in years as smelters scramble to secure ore regardless of cost.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: The rent has moved to miners; favor upstream copper producers over midstream smelters in the current cycle.
insights.quote: Profit margins have shifted upstream toward mines, giving mining companies their strongest bargaining position in years
insights.locator: To feed newly added capacity or fill raw material shortfalls

insights.claim: Copper smelters cannot simply cut output when losses emerge because continuous production is constrained by long-term contracts, equipment stability, sulfuric acid production lines, cash flow, and local GDP output targets.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Rigidity means overcapacity clears slowly, so negative TCs can persist far longer than simple margin logic would predict.
insights.quote: make it difficult for smelters to shut down furnaces on a large scale quickly, even when processing margins shrink to minimal levels
insights.locator: Xu Pengfei explained that the industry does not follow

insights.claim: Local GDP output targets are an explicit reason Chinese smelters keep furnaces running at minimal or negative margins, tying industrial policy to the persistence of overcapacity.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Policy-driven production floors mean market signals alone won't rationalize capacity; expect state incentives, not margins, to govern any output cuts.
insights.quote: meeting local GDP output targets make it difficult for smelters to shut down furnaces
insights.locator: Constraints such as fulfilling long-term contracts

themes.theme: By-Product Economics as the New Profit Engine

insights.claim: Robust earnings from by-products including gold, silver, antimony, germanium and sulfuric acid provide a substantial profit buffer that lets smelters tolerate extremely low or negative processing fees.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Smelter survival now hinges on precious- and minor-metal and acid prices; a downturn in those by-products would expose the negative-TC core and force capacity cuts.
insights.quote: robust earnings from these by-products provide a substantial profit buffer for smelters, enabling them to tolerate extremely low or even negative processing fees
insights.locator: Over the past two years, prices of precious metals

insights.claim: CRU data shows processing fees fell from 39% of smelters' total revenue in 2018 to a minor share, while free metals and by-product revenue (primarily sulfuric acid) became the top two income streams at 50-53% and 25-27% respectively.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: The revenue mix has fundamentally re-based; model smelter earnings off by-product and free-metal streams, not treatment charges.
insights.quote: processing fees accounted for 39% of smelters' total revenue back in 2018
insights.locator: Analysts at UK-based commodities research consultancy CRU

insights.claim: With copper prices hovering above the $10,000-per-ton threshold, midstream players are suffering a severe profit squeeze that reflects a fundamental mismatch between global mine supply and smelting demand.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: High copper prices coexisting with midstream distress confirms the bottleneck is concentrate supply, not demand; the trade is long the resource, cautious on processors.
insights.quote: As copper prices hover above the $10,000-per-ton threshold, midstream players across the industrial chain are suffering a severe profit squeeze
insights.locator: As copper prices hover above the $10,000-per-ton threshold
